title: KAPHE: Kernel-Aware Performance Heuristic Extraction - A Lightweight Statistical Methodology for Interpretable OS Kernel Configuration
description: We propose KAPHE, a statistical methodology that profiles application workloads to characterize their sensitivity to Linux kernel configurations, then extracts interpretable IF-THEN rules mapping workload characteristics to optimal kernel settings. Unlike ML-based approaches (MLKAPS, GPTune) that use black-box models, or LLM-based approaches (AutoOS, OS-R1) that require expensive inference, KAPHE uses statistical analysis and rule induction (RIPPER) to generate human-readable recommendations with minimal runtime overhead.
motivation: Linux kernel tuning remains challenging despite thousands of tunable parameters significantly impacting performance. Current ML-based approaches (MLKAPS 2025 for HPC kernels) use black-box decision trees lacking interpretability. LLM-based approaches (AutoOS 2024, OS-R1 2025) require expensive inference and face hallucination issues. We need a lightweight, interpretable approach that provides actionable insights without black-box models or LLM deployment costs.
proposed_approach: KAPHE operates in three phases: (1) Profiling - use eBPF to collect workload signatures across kernel subsystems (memory, I/O, scheduling) while varying runtime-tunable configurations via sysctl; (2) Characterization - apply statistical analysis (ANOVA, effect size, correlation) to identify which workload metrics predict performance under different configurations; (3) Extraction - use rule induction (RIPPER algorithm) to generate interpretable IF-THEN rules mapping workload characteristics to optimal settings with confidence and coverage metrics.
key_innovations: Systematic workload-to-kernel-configuration mapping for OS kernel (vs. HPC kernels in MLKAPS)
Interpretable rule extraction using statistical methods instead of black-box ML models
Zero runtime ML/LLM inference cost - simple rule matching at deployment
Explicit interpretability metrics (rule complexity, coverage, fidelity) vs. vague 'interpretability scores'
hypothesis: A statistical methodology that profiles application workloads across kernel configuration dimensions and extracts decision rules based on workload characteristics can achieve performance within 10% of optimal with minimal overhead (<3%), while providing actionable, interpretable guidance that outperforms black-box ML approaches in explainability.

success_criteria.primary: KAPHE achieves within 10% of oracle-optimal performance on 80%+ of diverse workloads
secondary: Profiling overhead remains below 3% for all workloads
Rule complexity: ≤50 total conditions across all rules
Rule fidelity ≥80% on held-out test workloads
Cross-workload generalization within categories exceeds 70% accuracy

interpretability_metrics.rule_complexity: Number of rules + average conditions per rule
interpretability_metrics.coverage: Percentage of test cases covered by extracted rules
interpretability_metrics.fidelity: Agreement between rule recommendations and oracle on test set
interpretability_metrics.rule_length: Average number of conditions per rule (target ≤5)

failure_conditions: Workload sensitivity to kernel configurations is found to be essentially random
Rule extraction produces trivial or unusable recommendations
Overhead exceeds 5% threshold for production deployment
Rules are less interpretable than simple decision tree baselines

differentiation_from_mlkaps.mlkaps_domain: HPC compute kernels (Intel MKL matrix operations) with algorithmic parameters
differentiation_from_mlkaps.kaphe_domain: Linux OS kernel with runtime-tunable system parameters via sysctl
differentiation_from_mlkaps.mlkaps_method: GBDT surrogate models + decision tree generation
differentiation_from_mlkaps.mlkaps_output: Embedded C code decision trees (low interpretability)
differentiation_from_mlkaps.kaphe_output: Human-readable IF-THEN rules with confidence/coverage metrics

related_work.paper: MLKAPS: Machine Learning and Adaptive Sampling for HPC Kernel Auto-tuning
related_work.difference: MLKAPS targets HPC kernels using GBDT and generates black-box decision trees. KAPHE targets OS kernel using statistical rule extraction with explicit interpretability metrics.

related_work.paper: AutoOS: Make Your OS More Powerful by Exploiting Large Language Models
related_work.difference: AutoOS uses LLMs with state machine traversal requiring expensive inference. KAPHE uses deterministic rule matching with no LLM deployment cost.

related_work.paper: OS-R1: Agentic Operating System Kernel Tuning with Reinforcement Learning
related_work.difference: OS-R1 uses RL-trained LLMs requiring GPU infrastructure. KAPHE requires no training and minimal runtime overhead.

related_work.paper: GPTune: Multitask Learning for Autotuning Exascale Applications
related_work.difference: GPTune uses Gaussian Processes for HPC applications. KAPHE uses rule induction for OS kernel tuning with explicit interpretability.

related_work.paper: An Experimental Comparison Analysis of Kernel-level Memory Allocators
related_work.difference: Provides empirical comparison but no predictive methodology. KAPHE provides systematic characterization and rule extraction.
Foundational RIPPER algorithm. KAPHE applies RIPPER to OS kernel tuning with novel interpretability metrics.

baselines: Oracle (grid search of feasible configuration space)

real_validation.description: Real kernel validation on 4 representative workloads (1 per category) using actual sysctl-tunable parameters
real_validation.purpose: Validate simulation results against ground truth measurements

feasibility.computational_requirements: CPU-only workload. Requires running benchmarks and statistical analysis. No GPU needed.
resources_needed: Python scientific stack (scipy, scikit-learn for RIPPER)
